title: Quantitative distributional aspects of generic diagonal forms
speaker: Jean Bourgain
affiliation: IBM von Neumann Professor, School of Mathematics

proof_stages.label: Quantitative Oppenheim-type framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk contrasts qualitative ergodic theorems on values of irrational ternary quadratic forms with the quantitative question of how small a window delta(N) suffices to approximate prescribed real values by Q(x) with ||x|| bounded by N.

proof_stages.label: One-parameter generic ternary diagonal theorem
proof_stages.summary: For the diagonal family x_1^2+alpha_2 x_2^2-alpha_3 x_3^2 with fixed alpha_2 and almost all alpha_3, a Lindelof-conditional bound achieves the optimal scale about N^{-1+epsilon}, with weaker unconditional variants and volume heuristics explaining optimality.

proof_stages.label: Spectral-spacing reduction to indefinite forms
proof_stages.summary: Eigenvalues of flat and rectangular tori are expressed as quadratic forms on lattice points, so pair correlations and minimal gaps reduce to quantitative distribution of differences of form values, while existing deterministic ergodic results do not control the needed shrinking windows.

proof_stages.label: Generic spacing theorems for billiards and diagonal forms
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states measure-generic quantitative results for small gaps of two-dimensional rectangular billiards, pair correlation of three-dimensional rectangular billiards in small windows, and Poisson statistics for higher-degree diagonal homogeneous forms with power savings.

proof_stages.label: Multiplicative Fourier--Dirichlet-polynomial method
proof_stages.summary: The central idea is to rewrite additive small-value inequalities multiplicatively using logarithms, apply Fourier analysis to obtain Dirichlet polynomials, separate low frequencies giving the continuous main term from high frequencies, and control the latter by averaging in the parameter and using Parseval plus Lindelof-type bounds.

proof_stages.label: Unconditional large-value and Epstein-zeta estimates
proof_stages.summary: To go beyond Lindelof, the proof invokes standard analytic number theory tools such as Jutila large-value inequalities for general Dirichlet polynomials and Titchmarsh/van der Corput exponent-pair bounds for partial Epstein zeta sums.

proof_stages.label: Small-gap proof via product variables
proof_stages.summary: For rectangular billiard minimal gaps, the signature (2,2) quadratic difference is factored into products, Fourier-expanded in logarithmic variables, and then refined by splitting variables into short and long factors so that large-value estimates control the high-frequency error and give the near N^{-2} scale without Lindelof.

proof_stages.label: Non-Poisson obstructions and open multiplicative approximation
proof_stages.summary: Ford's multiplication-table theorem is used to explain logarithmic violations of naive Poisson minimal-gap behavior, and the talk ends by isolating an even simpler open problem asking whether ||alpha mn|| can always beat the trivial 1/M scale.

examples.name: Concrete irrational ternary quadratic form
examples.note: The form x^2+y^2-a z^2 is used only to make the class of indefinite irrational ternary quadratic forms in the Dani--Margulis theorem concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker rewrites the specific ternary form x1^2+x2^2−alpha x3^2, sets up the small-value inequality, converts it to logarithms/Fourier integrals, and estimates the resulting terms

examples.name: Poisson-process eigenvalue model
examples.note: Treating local eigenvalues as points of a Poisson process is used heuristically to derive the expected pair-correlation normalization and the 1/N minimal-gap scale, though the proofs do not use an actual random process.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker derives the Poisson model’s predicted pair-correlation normalization and minimal-gap scale, explaining why the expected spacing is order one or 1/n in the relevant settings

passes.reason: The homogeneous Poisson process is used as the standard conjectural local-statistics model, not as an obstruction or classification exception.

examples.name: One-dimensional Titchmarsh/exponent-pair comparison
examples.note: The familiar one-variable zeta-sum estimate and the exponent pair (1/6,2/3) are recalled to explain the analogous Epstein-zeta estimate used in higher-dimensional bounds.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes how the one-dimensional exponent-pair/Titchmarsh-type bound gives powers such as t^(1/6) and n^(2/3), then compares this with the two-dimensional Epstein-zeta estimate

examples.name: Littlewood-conjecture analogy
examples.note: The final open problem for ||alpha mn|| is compared with Littlewood's conjecture to emphasize that it is an apparently simpler multiplicative Diophantine-approximation problem still out of reach.
